items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: Rutherford's gold foil experiment showed atoms have:
items.why: Correct answer: a small dense nucleus. “a uniform positive charge” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is most reactive: Li, Na, K or Rb?
items.why: Correct answer: Rb. “Li” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Atomic number of an element with electron config 2,8,7?
items.why: Exact answer: 17. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
